title: Cold High-Half Multiply Unit
motivation:
The current best result came from moving cold DIV/REM hardware out of the
universal EX-stage ALU path: CoreMark cycles barely changed, while median
Fmax rose sharply and LUT pressure dropped. The same shape remains in the
multiply path, but with a narrower target. `rtl/alu.sv` still builds three
independent 64-bit products every cycle for MUL, MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU.
CoreMark's timed kernels need ordinary low-half MUL, but high-half multiply
variants are normally absent or cold in this benchmark. The previous 33x33
shared-multiplier rewrite proved that reshaping the hot MUL primitive itself
can badly hurt placement, so the better micro-optimization is to leave the
existing low-half MUL fast path intact and remove only the cold high-half
multiplier variants from the one-cycle ALU.
hypothesis:
Keep ALU_MUL as the only single-cycle hardware multiply in the hot ALU path,
using the existing unsigned 32x32 product low half. Move MULH, MULHU, and
MULHSU into a small long-latency `mulh_unit` launched only for those three
opcodes. The unit can use an iterative shift/add absolute-value multiplier
with final sign correction, or another compact implementation that avoids
placing signed high-half products on the ALU result mux. EX should reuse the
existing long-operation interlock pattern from the divider: latch the original
instruction metadata and forwarded operands at launch, emit valid=0 bubbles
while busy, and inject the completed high-half result into EX/MEM on done.
Under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, the MULH/MULHU/MULHSU substitute formulas should
live in the new unit with a short start/busy/done latency so formal depth
remains tractable. This should preserve CoreMark iterations/cycle because
hot low-half MUL remains one-cycle, while improving FPGA Fmax and placement
by eliminating two cold wide multiplier products and their result-mux routing
from the common EX datapath.

changes.description:
Remove the signed*signed and signed*unsigned product expressions from the
combinational ALU. Keep only the existing unsigned product needed for
single-cycle ALU_MUL low-half results, and make ALU_MULH, ALU_MULHU, and
ALU_MULHSU produce a dummy value on the hot ALU output because those
opcodes will be completed by the new long-latency unit. Preserve the
current ALTOPS behavior for ALU_MUL, and move the high-half ALTOPS
substitutions to the new unit.

Add a compact high-half multiply unit with start, busy, done, op,
operands, and result signals. It must implement exact RV32M semantics for
MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU, including signedness and sign correction, while
avoiding a second and third always-live wide multiplier on the EX ALU
path. In RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS builds, return the existing high-half
substitute formulas with a short bounded latency.

Detect MULH/MULHU/MULHSU in EX, launch `mulh_unit` once with the
post-forwarded operands, and OR its activity into the existing
`ex_long_busy` interlock. While the unit is active, bubble EX/MEM so no
older instruction is replayed or retired twice. On completion, latch the
original instruction metadata, rd, rs values, pc_next, control fields,
and completed high-half result into EX/MEM, matching the divider's RVFI
ordering discipline.

implementation_notes:
# Cold High-Half Multiply Unit

## What changed

- `rtl/alu.sv` now keeps only the hot low-half `ALU_MUL` path. `ALU_MULH`, `ALU_MULHU`, and `ALU_MULHSU` return the same dummy zero value as other long-latency M ops on the ALU output.
- Added `rtl/mulh_unit.sv`, a long-latency high-half multiply unit for `MULH`, `MULHU`, and `MULHSU`. The real implementation uses 32-cycle unsigned shift/add multiplication over absolute values plus final two's-complement sign correction. The `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` branch returns the prior high-half substitute formulas with divider-like short bounded latency.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv` now detects high-half multiply ops, launches `mulh_unit` with post-forwarded operands, stalls IF/ID through `ex_long_busy`, bubbles EX/MEM while busy, and injects the completed result with the latched original instruction metadata and RVFI operand values.
- Updated cocotb tests so `test_alu.py` covers the remaining ALU surface, added `test_mulh_unit.py` for exact high-half arithmetic, and added a pipeline interlock/forwarding case for MULH-family ops.

## Deviations

- The implementation follows the hypothesis directly. The only practical deviation is verification workflow: `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree was blocked by the sandbox before SBY started because the script stages files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/...` and that path could not be cleaned (`Operation not permitted`). I ran the same script logic from a temporary `/tmp` copy with the riscv-formal directory renamed so staging was writable and the checked-in `formal/` tree remained untouched.

## Concerns

- High-half multiply now has 32-cycle real latency, so any workload with frequent `MULH`/`MULHU`/`MULHSU` will slow down. This matches the hypothesis assumption that those opcodes are cold for the fitness workload.
- No known functional concerns after lint, targeted cocotb, and formal.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `pytest -q test/test_mulh_unit.py test/test_pipeline.py`: pass, 2 passed.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked by sandbox during staging cleanup before SBY.
- Equivalent temporary formal run using the same `run_all.sh` logic and current RTL: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Single-Entry Retiring Store Buffer
motivation:
The current best core gained a large Fmax and LUT win by moving DIV/REM out
of the hot EX path, so the next structural bottleneck is less likely to be
raw datapath area and more likely to be cycles lost to the random dmem
handshake model. Today every dmem-not-ready cycle for a STORE freezes the
whole pipeline, even though the store address, replicated data, byte mask,
and RVFI memory metadata are already known in MEM and the store produces no
register result for younger ALU/control instructions. A prior two-entry
store buffer proved the idea through formal and cosim but failed placement
on the larger pre-DIV RTL. A smaller one-entry RAM-store buffer should retain
most of the useful stall hiding while keeping the arbitration, state, and
placement pressure low enough for the post-DIV design.
hypothesis:
Add one in-order retiring store slot for legal aligned stores to normal RAM
only. When such a store reaches MEM and io_dmemReady is low, capture its
byte address, replicated write data, byte mask, and RVFI-visible memory
fields into the slot, retire the store once into MEM/WB, and allow younger
non-memory instructions to continue while the slot drains on the external
dmem port. The buffered store has priority on the dmem port until accepted.
Younger loads, younger stores, and all MMIO/marker/UART stores must wait
behind the slot so memory ordering and timing-marker semantics stay exact.
If the slot is full, or if the store is misaligned or nonbufferable, the core
falls back to the current full dmem hold behavior. This targets store-only
backpressure under the 22% random dStall model without adding load data to
the EX operand muxes, changing branch prediction, widening retirement, or
weakening RVFI ordering.

changes.description:
Add a one-entry oldest-first store slot with enqueue, valid, full,
drain-valid, and drain-ready controls. The entry holds the byte address,
replicated write data, byte mask, and any compact bookkeeping needed for
dmem arbitration; it never buffers MMIO, marker, UART, misaligned, load,
or trapping accesses.

Split store handling into bufferable RAM stores and nonbufferable stores.
A bufferable aligned store may advance to MEM/WB when accepted by the
empty store slot even if io_dmemReady is low; loads, MMIO stores,
marker/UART stores, misaligned accesses, and stores when the slot is
full keep the existing wait-for-handshake behavior. RVFI mem_addr,
mem_wmask, mem_wdata, trap, and valid fields remain tied to the retiring
store, not to the later drain cycle.

Replace the current any-memory-op dmem hold predicate with explicit MEM
backpressure inputs: load waiting for dmem, nonbufferable store waiting
for dmem or an older buffered store, and bufferable store unable to
enqueue because the slot is full. Do not freeze younger non-memory work
solely because a buffered store is draining.

Instantiate the store slot and arbitrate it onto the top-level dmem port.
Give the buffered store priority while it is draining, block younger
memory operations behind it, and preserve the existing top-level core IO,
single-retire channel-0 RVFI behavior, strict rvfi_order increments, and
tied-off channel 1.

Add only small packed helper fields or comments if needed to carry
bufferable-store and MEM-stage backpressure state cleanly between MEM,
hazard, and top-level arbitration. Do not change architectural state,
RVFI field widths, decoder legality rules, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added `rtl/store_buffer.sv`, a one-entry oldest-first store drain slot that holds byte address, replicated write data, and byte mask.
- Updated `rtl/mem_stage.sv` to classify bufferable stores as valid, legal, aligned, normal-RAM stores with byte address `< 0x00100000`.
- A bufferable store enqueues and retires through MEM/WB when `io_dmemReady` is low and the slot is empty. Its RVFI memory fields remain attached to that retiring MEM/WB instruction.
- Loads, stores behind a full slot, MMIO/marker/UART stores, non-RAM stores, and misaligned/nonbufferable stores produce explicit MEM backpressure instead of relying on a generic `any memory op && !dmemReady` predicate.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` to arbitrate the buffered store onto the dmem port with priority until accepted. Live MEM-stage dmem requests use the port only when the store slot is idle.
- Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` to consume explicit `mem_load_wait` / `mem_store_wait` inputs, allowing younger non-memory instructions to keep retiring while a buffered RAM store drains.
- Added `store_buffer.sv` to the cocotb pipeline test source list in `test/test_pipeline.py`.

## Deviations

- No core package type changes were needed.
- The store buffer does not hold RVFI bookkeeping because the design retires the store at enqueue time and only drains the external write request later.

## Concerns

- The riscv-formal wrapper ties `io_dmemReady` high, so the new enqueue/drain path is not meaningfully exercised by formal. It is covered structurally by lint and will be exercised by the orchestrator's random-dStall cosim.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree could not refresh the symlinked shared `formal/riscv-formal` staging area under this sandbox (`Operation not permitted`). I copied `formal/` and `rtl/` to `/tmp/aa-formal-r7s1-36704` and ran the same script there so all generated files were under a writable path.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from `/tmp/aa-formal-r7s1-36704`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- `pytest -q test/test_pipeline.py`: pass, `1 passed`.

title: One-Entry Return Target Predictor
motivation:
The current best RTL already predicts backward conditional branches and
direct JAL targets in IF, and the latest winning change moved cold DIV/REM
off the critical ALU path. The predictor slot should therefore avoid broad
learned branch tables that have already shown a bad cycle-count versus Fmax
tradeoff: the 64-entry agree table and 16-entry forward override both reduced
CoreMark cycles but lost more clock than they recovered.

The remaining high-value predictor miss is JALR return control flow. The
4-entry RAS experiment showed returns exist in the timed path, but its stack
bookkeeping and IF routing were still too expensive. A single tagged return
target entry is a narrower bet: it targets recurring canonical return PCs
with one PC tag, one target register, and one IF compare, avoiding a BTB,
multi-entry stack, or conditional-branch table.
hypothesis:
Add a one-entry return target predictor for canonical return JALRs. When EX
resolves a valid, non-trapping `jalr x0, 0(x1)` or `jalr x0, 0(x5)`, record
that return instruction PC and the resolved next PC in a single valid/tag/
target entry. When IF later fetches the same canonical return PC and the entry
is valid, predict the cached target through the existing `predicted_taken` and
`predicted_target` metadata path.

JALR redirect generation should become resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery,
just like the existing predicted direct-JAL path. Uncached or incorrectly
cached JALRs still redirect in EX because their carried prediction is pc+4 or
the wrong target; correctly cached returns do not flush. If a predicted JALR
resolves as a misaligned-target trap, EX must redirect back to the trap
fallback next PC of pc+4 so RVFI pc_wdata and the next retiring pc_rdata stay
consistent. The expected result is a small CoreMark cycle reduction with much
lower Fmax risk than the prior RAS or learned conditional-branch predictors.

changes.description:
Add one valid/tag/target return-predictor entry updated synchronously from
EX. Recognize canonical return JALR encodings in IF, compare the fetched
PC against the cached tag, and when prediction is enabled choose the
cached target as next_pc while emitting the existing predicted_taken and
predicted_target metadata. Preserve reset, EX redirect, stall, backward
branch, and direct-JAL prediction priority.

Detect valid canonical return JALRs after operand forwarding and emit a
one-cycle update containing the return PC and resolved target only when
the EX/MEM register advances and the instruction is not a misaligned trap.
Change JALR redirect logic from always-redirect to resolved-next-PC
mismatch, including pc+4 as the resolved next PC for misaligned target
traps so wrong cached return predictions are repaired.

Wire the EX-stage return-predictor update signals back to IF without
changing the top-level core IO, RVFI NRET=2 port layout, retirement order,
channel-0-only single retirement behavior, or tied-off channel 1.

Clarify comments for predicted_taken and predicted_target as generic
next-PC prediction metadata covering conditional branches, direct JALs,
and the one-entry cached return predictor. Do not change bundle widths,
RVFI fields, decoder legality rules, or architectural state semantics.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added a one-entry cached return target predictor in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
  - The entry stores valid/tag/target state.
  - IF recognizes canonical `jalr x0, 0(x1)` and `jalr x0, 0(x5)` encodings.
  - A matching valid entry drives the existing `predicted_taken` and `predicted_target` metadata path.
- Changed `rtl/ex_stage.sv` JALR redirect behavior from unconditional redirect to resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery.
  - Jumps now compare the carried prediction against the resolved next PC.
  - Misaligned jump targets resolve to `pc+4`, so a wrong cached return prediction is redirected back to the trap fallback PC.
  - Canonical, valid, non-trapping returns emit a one-cycle predictor update with the return instruction PC and resolved target when EX/MEM can advance.
- Wired the EX return-predictor update signals back to IF in `rtl/core.sv`.
- Clarified `predicted_taken` / `predicted_target` comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to cover backward branches, direct JALs, and cached-return JALRs.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis.
- I did not add an invalidation path for a stale return entry on misaligned-target traps; EX repairs those cases by redirecting to `pc+4`, and non-trapping canonical returns overwrite the entry.

## Concerns

- The predictor is intentionally one-entry and PC-tagged, so alternating return PCs will overwrite each other and fall back to EX recovery.
- The direct in-worktree `bash formal/run_all.sh` command could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected location outside the writable sandbox. It failed before invoking the solver with `Operation not permitted` on the script's cleanup of staged `.sv` files.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from a writable `/tmp` mirror of the same `rtl/` and `formal/` inputs: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Reset-Valid LUTRAM Register File
motivation:
The post-DIV best core has removed the largest cold arithmetic block from
the EX critical path, so the next micro-optimization should target FPGA
placement pressure without changing CoreMark cycles. The current register
file is a 32x32 array with a synchronous reset loop that clears every data
bit. That resettable array is likely implemented as ordinary flip-flops or
reset-muxed logic instead of compact distributed RAM, adding roughly 1024
resettable state bits plus reset fanout to the already timing-sensitive
fetch/decode/register-read region.

Architectural software only needs the externally visible reset behavior of
reading zero before a register has been written. It does not require the
physical register data array itself to be synchronously cleared. A valid-zero
mask can preserve the current tests and reset semantics while allowing the
32x32 data storage to become resetless.
hypothesis:
Replace the register file's full 1024-bit synchronous data clear with a
resettable 32-bit written/valid mask. On reset, clear only the mask. On a
nonzero register write, update the resetless data array and set that
register's valid bit. Reads still return zero for x0, same-cycle write data
for write-first bypasses, and otherwise either the stored data when the valid
bit is set or zero when it is clear.

This keeps the architectural and unit-test-visible reset-clears-all behavior,
preserves x0 hardwiring and write-first bypassing, and does not change any
pipeline timing, RVFI fields, retirement order, memory behavior, or CoreMark
instruction count. The expected gain is a lower FF/reset burden and a better
chance for Yosys/nextpnr to map the data array into LUTRAM or simpler
unreset flops, improving median Fmax with essentially no IPC risk.

changes.description:
Remove the synchronous reset loop over the 32x32 data array. Add a
resettable per-register valid mask, set valid bits on nonzero writes,
and gate read data to zero when a register has not been written since
reset. Preserve the existing same-cycle write-first bypass priority and
x0 hardwired-zero behavior, and keep the module port list unchanged.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/reg_file.sv` so the 32x32 data array is no longer synchronously reset.
- Added a resettable 32-bit `valid_q` mask.
- On reset, only `valid_q` is cleared.
- On a nonzero write, the data array entry is updated and the destination valid bit is set.
- Reads preserve the existing priority:
  - x0 returns zero.
  - A same-cycle write to the read address returns `w_data`.
  - Otherwise, a valid register returns stored data.
  - An invalid register returns zero.

## Deviations

- No module ports, pipeline timing, RVFI wiring, or surrounding RTL were changed.
- The read mux also returns zero while `reset` is asserted. This preserves the previous externally visible reset behavior without adding reset fanout to the data array.

## Concerns

- None known. The physical data array is intentionally left uninitialized after reset, but reads are gated by the valid mask until a register is written.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `pytest -q test/test_reg_file.py`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass in a writable `/tmp` copy of the worktree, with `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

Directly running `bash formal/run_all.sh` from this worktree was blocked before any formal checks ran because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable sandbox, and the script refreshes `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher` by removing staged `.sv` files. I reran the same command from `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-r8s0` after dereferencing that symlink into a writable copy.

title: One-Deep Store Retirement Slot With Direct Fast Path
motivation:
The current best core is dominated by the post-DIV/REM timing shape, with
median Fmax now high enough that broad datapath changes have mostly hurt
placement. The remaining structural opportunity with measured evidence is
store backpressure: the previous one-entry store buffer reduced CoreMark
cycles from 4,259,618 to 4,226,169, but its dmem arbitration and MEM-stage
muxing cost slightly more Fmax than the cycle gain recovered.

The promising angle is therefore not a larger buffer, but a smaller and more
timing-local retiring store slot. Keep the zero-wait store path identical to
the current best design, and add state only for the uncommon case where a
normal RAM store reaches MEM while io_dmemReady is low. That targets the same
random-dStall cycles as the prior structural attempt while avoiding a
permanent dmem-port mux on every load/store cycle.
hypothesis:
Add a one-deep pending-store retirement slot that is used only on a legal,
aligned normal-RAM store when io_dmemReady is low. When io_dmemReady is high
and the slot is empty, stores drive the existing dmem outputs directly and
retire exactly as they do today. When io_dmemReady is low, the MEM stage
captures the store address, replicated write data, byte mask, and RVFI memory
metadata into the slot, retires that store once through MEM/WB, and lets
younger non-memory instructions continue while the slot drains.

The slot has priority only while valid. Younger loads, younger stores, and all
MMIO/marker/UART stores stall until the pending store drains, preserving
in-order memory visibility and exact timing-marker behavior. Misaligned or
trapping stores never enter the slot. This should keep most of the cycle-count
benefit seen in the previous store-buffer experiment, but recover Fmax by
leaving the direct ready-store/load path structurally close to the current
best RTL and confining the extra muxing to the stall-only drain path.

changes.description:
Add a one-entry pending-store slot with valid, enqueue, drain-valid, and
drain-ready controls. The entry holds byte address, replicated write data,
and byte mask for legal aligned normal-RAM stores only. It is allocated
only when the live store cannot handshake immediately.

Split store completion into a direct fast path and a low-ready enqueue
path. Keep the current dmem outputs and MEM/WB behavior unchanged when
io_dmemReady is high and no pending store exists. On a bufferable store
with io_dmemReady low, enqueue the precomputed store fields, retire the
store once with correct RVFI mem_addr, mem_wmask, and mem_wdata, and
report explicit MEM backpressure for loads, nonbufferable stores, stores
behind a valid slot, and full-slot cases.

Replace the generic dmem-stall predicate with MEM-stage wait inputs that
distinguish live loads/nonbufferable stores from a draining pending store.
Stall IF/ID/EX only when the instruction in MEM truly cannot retire or
when a younger memory operation must wait behind the slot; allow younger
non-memory instructions to proceed while the slot drains.

Instantiate the pending-store slot and arbitrate the top-level dmem port
so the live MEM-stage request uses the direct path whenever the slot is
empty, while the slot drives the port only when valid. Preserve the top
module IO, RVFI NRET=2 fields, strict channel-0 retirement ordering, and
tied-off channel 1.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added `rtl/store_slot.sv`, a one-entry pending-store slot that holds byte address, replicated write data, and byte mask until `io_dmemReady` accepts the write.
- Split the MEM-stage dmem path into a live direct request and an enqueue path. Legal, aligned normal-RAM stores enqueue only when `io_dmemReady` is low and the slot is empty; ready stores keep the existing direct path.
- Added precise `mem_wait` classification in `rtl/mem_stage.sv` for live loads, nonbufferable stores, and younger memory operations blocked behind a valid slot.
- Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` to consume `mem_wait` instead of rebuilding `!dmem_ready && ex_mem_mem_op`, allowing younger non-memory instructions to continue while the slot drains.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` to instantiate the slot and arbitrate the top-level dmem port with slot priority while valid.
- Updated the cocotb pipeline source list to include `store_slot.sv`.

## Deviations

- The slot stores the physical drain fields only. RVFI memory metadata for an enqueued store is still generated and retired through the normal MEM/WB register on the enqueue cycle, so there is no second RVFI event when the slot drains.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not run in-place in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a read-only path outside the writable worktree, and the script failed while removing stale staged files. I copied the same formal tree, wrapper, checks config, and current `rtl/*.sv` into `/tmp/codex-formal-r8s1.FkHGdP` and ran the unchanged `formal/run_all.sh` there.

## Concerns

- The riscv-formal wrapper ties `io_dmemReady` high, so formal proves the unchanged direct path but does not exercise pending-slot drain behavior. The slot path is covered by lint and structural review here; downstream random-dStall cosim remains the main gate for that behavior.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from writable `/tmp` mirror: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- `pytest -q test/test_pipeline.py`: pass, `1 passed`.

title: Funct3-Biased Static Branch Predictor
motivation:
The current best frontend already has the two predictor features that have
actually paid off: backward conditional branches predict taken, and direct
JAL targets are predicted in IF. The learned predictor attempts point in the
other direction: both the 64-entry agree table and the 16-entry forward-only
override table reduced CoreMark cycles, but their table read/update routing
cost more Fmax than the saved mispredicts recovered. Return prediction was
even less attractive after the post-DIV timing win, with only small cycle
savings and a large clock regression.

The remaining promising predictor angle is therefore a zero-state refinement
of the static conditional-branch rule. A quick dynamic profile of the timed
CoreMark region shows that the baseline BTFNT rule is especially wrong for
two simple branch classes: backward BEQ branches, which are mostly not taken
in this binary, and forward BNE branches, which are usually taken. Those two
classes account for a large fraction of residual static predictor mistakes,
and they can be recognized from the funct3 bits already examined in IF for
branch legality. This targets iterations/cycle without adding a predictor
RAM, update feedback path, BTB, RAS, or extra target adder.
hypothesis:
Replace the current pure BTFNT conditional-branch direction rule with a
small funct3-biased static rule: legal aligned backward branches still
predict taken except backward BEQ, which predicts not taken; legal aligned
forward branches still predict not taken except forward BNE, which predicts
taken. Direct JAL prediction remains unchanged, and JALR remains unpredicted.

The IF stage already computes the branch immediate sign, target alignment,
opcode, and funct3 legality before choosing next_pc. The implementation
should only add two simple funct3 equality terms to the branch prediction
condition and continue to carry the existing predicted_taken and
predicted_target metadata into EX. EX recovery stays the current resolved
next-PC mismatch check, so any static-bias mistake only costs the normal
redirect bubbles and cannot weaken RVFI pc_wdata, trap behavior, retirement
ordering, or the NRET=2 IO contract. The expected benefit is a modest but
low-risk CoreMark cycle reduction with far less Fmax exposure than the
previous table-based predictor experiments.

changes.description:
Refine the conditional-branch prediction expression. Keep the existing
B-type opcode, funct3 legality, target-alignment, reset/flush/redirect,
and stall guards. For conditional branches, predict taken when the
immediate is negative and funct3 is not BEQ, or when the immediate is
non-negative and funct3 is BNE. Preserve direct-JAL prediction priority
and the current predicted_taken/predicted_target metadata behavior.

Update the IF/ID and ID/EX prediction comments to describe the refined
static branch-bias rule. Do not change bundle widths, RVFI fields,
decoder legality defaults, architectural state, or top-level IO.

- Direct `JAL` prediction priority and target selection are unchanged. `JALR` remains unpredicted.

title: Hot-Cold ALU Opcode Split
motivation:
The current best design already moved DIV/REM out of the universal EX
datapath and added the one-deep store slot, so the remaining micro-optimum
is likely in the hot single-cycle EX logic rather than in another stall
hiding structure. `rtl/alu.sv` still selects every RV32I operation and all
multiply-class results through one dense ALU opcode case. Even though
DIV/REM now return dummy zero in the ALU, the common ADD/address/compare/
shift path still shares decode and mux structure with the cold M-extension
result cases.

A previous "hot-bit ALU" attempt was never architecturally evaluated because
the implementation touched an off-limits non-RTL helper file. The idea
remains attractive after the divider win: leave every arithmetic primitive
and all pipeline control unchanged, but make the opcode encoding expose a
simple hot/cold split that synthesis can map into a shallower mux for the
common RV32I path. This targets median Fmax with no CoreMark cycle-count
risk and avoids the multiplier-primitive rewrites that previously regressed
badly.
hypothesis:
Re-encode the ALU operation constants so `alu_op[4]` is a registered
M-extension discriminator: all RV32I/simple operations keep `op[4]=0`, while
`MUL`, `MULH`, `MULHSU`, `MULHU`, `DIV`, `DIVU`, `REM`, and `REMU` use
`op[4]=1` with the low bits matching the RV32M funct3 order. Then refactor
`rtl/alu.sv` into two local result muxes: a hot simple mux for ADD/SUB,
logic, compare, shift, and LUI, and a cold M mux for the existing multiply
outputs plus the existing dummy zero result for DIV/REM opcodes handled by
`div_unit.sv`.

The final ALU result becomes `op[4] ? m_out : simple_out`. No multiplier,
divider, forwarding, stall, branch predictor, memory, RVFI, or top-level IO
behavior changes. The `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` multiply substitutions remain in
the cold M mux under the new constants, and the divide/remainder ALTOPS
formulas stay in `div_unit.sv`. The expected gain is a modest Fmax
improvement from reducing decode/mux depth on the hot EX ALU path while
preserving the current CoreMark instruction count and exact RV32IM results.

changes.description:
Reassign the ALU_MUL through ALU_REMU constants to an `op[4]=1`
encoding, preferably `5'b10000` through `5'b10111` in RV32M funct3
order. Keep ADD/SUB/logic/compare/shift/LUI at `op[4]=0`. Do not
change bundle widths, RVFI fields, architectural state, decoder legality
defaults, or the NRET=2 contract.

Split the broad ALU result case into `simple_out` and `m_out` sub-muxes.
`simple_out` should cover the RV32I hot path only. `m_out` should reuse
the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU arithmetic and ALTOPS formulas, while
DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU continue to return zero because the iterative divider
supplies their real result. Select the final output only with `op[4]`.

Keep the decoded operation semantics identical while emitting the new
ALU constants for RV32M instructions. The decoder's illegal-default
discipline and validated opcode/funct matching must remain unchanged.

Update any divide-class or M-extension equality checks to the new ALU
constants without changing the existing long-operation interlock, DIV
launch/completion behavior, forwarding muxes, redirect handling, or
RVFI metadata propagation.

Update opcode comparisons and ALTOPS divide/remainder case labels to the
new ALU constants only. Preserve the iterative divider protocol, real
RV32M corner-case behavior, and short formal ALTOPS latency.

title: Store-Slot-Aware Fetch Skid Queue
motivation:
The current best design combines the two changes that have paid off most:
cold DIV/REM is off the universal EX path, and a one-deep pending-store slot
lets a stalled normal-RAM store retire while the physical dmem write drains
later. That store slot reduced CoreMark cycles, but the frontend is still
structurally brittle under the tournament's random imem stalls: `if_stage`
presents live imem data directly to decode, and `hazard_unit` still turns
raw `io_imemReady=0` into a global pipeline stall.

A previous generic elastic fetch buffer regressed because it added permanent
frontend queue/arbitration cost before the divider and store-slot timing
wins, and it tried to prefetch through all backend stalls. The better
structural angle now is narrower: use a tiny fetch skid queue only to absorb
instruction-memory backpressure and to keep fetch productive while the new
store slot is draining for older stores. This targets the measured
store-slot cycle win's remaining companion bottleneck without touching the
hot EX operand path, load data forwarding, branch predictor state, or RVFI
retirement width.
hypothesis:
Add a two-entry instruction fetch skid queue between IF and ID with a direct
empty-queue bypass for the zero-wait no-stall path. The fetch side captures
`{pc, instr, predicted_taken, predicted_target}` whenever `io_imemReady` is
high and the queue has space. The decode side consumes exactly one entry
when ID is not stalled. When the queue is empty and both imem and decode are
ready, keep the current combinational bypass so the normal predicted
branch/JAL path does not gain an extra stage.

Unlike the earlier broad elastic-fetch experiment, restrict decoupling to
frontend availability and the existing pending-store drain case. Load-use
interlocks, live loads waiting on dmem, nonbufferable/MMIO stores, divide
long operations, and EX redirects still hold or flush the frontend exactly
as today. While a normal-RAM store has already retired into the pending
store slot and the slot is draining, allow IF to fill the skid queue and
allow younger non-memory work to consume from it; younger memory operations
still wait behind the slot in MEM, preserving memory order and marker
semantics.

Redirects flush all queued entries and restart fetch at the resolved target.
The existing backward-branch and direct-JAL prediction metadata is stored
per queue entry and is still checked in EX through the resolved-next-PC
mismatch path. The expected benefit is lower random-iStall exposure and
better overlap between store-slot drains and instruction fetch, with only a
small FF/LUT cost and no new load-data-to-EX or learned-predictor timing
path.

changes.description:
Add a two-entry valid/ready fetch skid queue holding the existing IF/ID
metadata. Preserve an empty-queue direct bypass from `io_imemData` to
decode when imem and ID are both ready. Push fetched entries only when
`io_imemReady` is high and the queue is not full; pop only when decode
accepts an instruction. Flush queued entries on reset or EX redirect,
and keep the existing static backward-branch plus direct-JAL prediction
logic on the fetch side.

Stop using raw `io_imemReady` as a global pipeline stall. Instead,
accept a frontend-empty/no-instruction-available indication from IF and
only bubble ID when the queue cannot provide an instruction. Keep the
existing load-use, divide-long-operation, redirect, and MEM wait
behavior intact, including stalls for live loads, nonbufferable stores,
and younger memory operations blocked behind the pending store slot.

Wire the fetch queue ready/valid controls between IF, ID, and the hazard
unit. Preserve the current pending-store slot arbitration, the top-level
core IO shape, single-retire RVFI channel-0 behavior, strict
`rvfi_order` increments, and tied-off channel 1.

Add or clarify only small IF/ID metadata comments or typedef fields
needed for the queued fetch entries. Do not change RVFI field widths,
architectural state, decoder legality defaults, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added a two-entry fetch skid queue inside `rtl/if_stage.sv` for `{pc, instr, predicted_taken, predicted_target, valid}`.
- Preserved the empty-queue direct bypass from live `io_imemData` to decode when `io_imemReady` is high.
- Fetch now advances the PC only when a live imem word is captured by the bypass or queue; when imem is stalled and the queue is empty, IF emits an invalid NOP bubble.
- Redirect/reset/flush discard queued entries and restart fetch from the resolved redirect target.
- `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` no longer consumes raw `io_imemReady`; it uses `if_id_w.valid` to gate load-use detection and leaves imem unavailability to the IF queue valid path.
- `rtl/core.sv` wires `io_imemReady` into IF, wires decode readiness for queue pop, and wires `if_id_w.valid` into the hazard unit.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv` comments now describe the IF/ID bundle as either bypassed or queued metadata.

## Deviations

- I did not add a new typedef for the fetch queue entry. The existing `if_id_t` already contains exactly the required metadata, including `valid`.
- I kept backend hold behavior conservative: load-use, long divide, and MEM waits still hold the fetch side instead of prefetching through those hazards. Store-slot drain only remains decoupled when it does not assert `mem_wait`, matching the existing pending-store ordering rule.

## Concerns

- The queue adds a small amount of IF-side control logic around the PC update path. The zero-wait empty-queue path still bypasses the queue storage, but synthesis/P&R will decide whether the added space/valid logic affects Fmax.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `pytest -q test/test_pipeline.py`: pass.
- Exact `bash formal/run_all.sh` from this worktree: blocked by sandbox permissions because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to `/Users/bonetto/bonetto/auto-arch-tournament/formal/riscv-formal`, and the script stages files by deleting/writing inside that external tree.

title: X1 Last-Call Return Predictor
motivation:
The current best RTL already has the predictor features that have proven
useful without excessive timing cost: backward conditional branches are
predicted taken and direct JAL targets are predicted in IF. Broader learned
direction tables did reduce CoreMark cycles, but the IF table read and EX
update routing cost more Fmax than the saved redirects recovered. The return
predictor attempts also showed that return bubbles exist, but the 4-entry
stack and the PC-tagged one-entry cache added too much frontend comparison,
bookkeeping, and routing.

The narrow remaining predictor opportunity is to keep only the useful part
of a return-address stack: the standard ABI link register x1. CoreMark is
compiled as ordinary RV32IM C code, so direct calls write x1 and returns are
canonical `jalr x0, 0(x1)`. A single last-call target register can predict
the common innermost return without a BTB, tag compare, multi-entry stack, or
conditional-branch table. It intentionally gives up nested-return coverage in
exchange for a much smaller IF critical-path footprint.
hypothesis:
Add a one-entry x1-only last-call return predictor. When EX accepts a valid,
non-trapping direct JAL call with `rd=x1`, record `pc+4` in a single
resettable return-target register and mark it valid. When EX accepts a valid
canonical return `jalr x0, 0(x1)`, clear that valid bit. In IF, recognize
only the exact canonical x1 return encoding and, when the valid bit is set,
predict the stored return target through the existing `predicted_taken` and
`predicted_target` metadata path.

Direct JAL prediction and backward-branch prediction remain unchanged, and
no table or PC tag is added. JALR redirect generation changes from
unconditional redirect to resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery, so an
unpredicted return behaves as it does today, an incorrectly predicted return
is repaired in EX, and a correctly predicted return does not flush. For a
misaligned JALR target trap, EX must treat the resolved next PC as `pc+4` and
redirect there if IF predicted the cached return target, preserving RVFI
`pc_wdata` and the next retired `pc_rdata` relationship. The expected gain is
a small CoreMark cycle reduction with lower Fmax risk than the prior RAS or
tagged return-target experiments.

changes.description:
Add one resettable valid bit and one 32-bit return-target register.
Accept EX update inputs for pushing `pc+4` on valid `rd=x1` direct JAL
calls and clearing the entry on valid canonical x1 returns. In IF,
recognize only `jalr x0, 0(x1)` and predict the stored target when the
entry is valid, prediction is enabled, and no reset/flush/redirect/stall
suppression is active. Keep the existing backward-branch and direct-JAL
prediction priority and metadata behavior otherwise.

Emit one-cycle last-call predictor update sideband signals when EX/MEM is
allowed to advance: push `pc+4` for valid non-misaligned direct JAL calls
writing x1, and clear the entry for valid canonical x1 returns. Change
JALR redirect logic to compare the resolved next PC against the carried
prediction, including the `pc+4` trap fallback for misaligned targets, so
cached return predictions are repaired precisely when wrong.

Wire the EX-stage last-call predictor update signals back to IF without
changing the top-level core IO, RVFI NRET=2 port layout, strict
channel-0 retirement ordering, or tied-off channel 1 behavior.

Clarify the prediction metadata comments to cover conditional branches,
direct JALs, and the x1-only last-call return predictor. Do not change
bundle widths, RVFI fields, decoder legality defaults, architectural
state, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What Changed

- Added a one-entry x1-only last-call return predictor in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
  The entry has a resettable valid bit and 32-bit target register.
- IF now recognizes only the exact canonical return encoding
  `jalr x0, 0(x1)` (`32'h0000_8067`) and predicts the stored target through
  the existing `predicted_taken` / `predicted_target` metadata path.
- EX now emits sideband update pulses when the current EX instruction is
  accepted into EX/MEM:
  - push `pc+4` for valid, non-trapping direct `JAL` calls with `rd=x1`
  - clear the entry for valid canonical x1 returns
- Changed EX control-flow repair to compare the carried prediction against a
  single resolved next PC for branches, direct JALs, and JALR. Misaligned
  branch/JAL/JALR traps resolve next PC as `pc+4`; a predicted JALR target is
  redirected back to `pc+4` when that trap path is taken.
- Wired the EX sideband update signals through `rtl/core.sv` with no top-level
  IO changes.
- Updated prediction metadata comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`; no bundle widths
  or RVFI fields changed.

## Deviations

- No table, tag, stack, or extra predictor metadata was added.
- I kept the EX update signals as synchronous predictor-state updates only and
  did not feed the clear pulse into IF's combinational prediction decision.
  This avoids adding an EX-to-IF timing path; any stale same-cycle return
  prediction is repaired by the resolved-next-PC mismatch logic.

## Concerns

- The predictor intentionally only covers the most recent x1 direct-call return.
  Nested returns and non-canonical return forms remain unpredicted.
- The stock repo-local `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not stage files in this
  sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout
  outside the writable worktree; it failed before running proofs with
  `Operation not permitted` during stale staged-file cleanup.

title: Collapse Redundant EX/MEM Metadata
motivation:
The current best RTL already captured the large available wins: cold
DIV/REM is out of the one-cycle ALU path, and the pending store slot reduces
random dmem-stall exposure. The remaining micro-opt slot should therefore
avoid new predictors, queues, or arithmetic units and instead trim logic that
is already proven unnecessary on the hot pipeline boundary.

The EX/MEM bundle still carries several fields that do not add architectural
information. `write_data` is always assigned the same post-forwarded rs2
value as `rs2_val`, while `branch_taken` and `branch_target` are registered
but never consumed by MEM, WB, RVFI, forwarding, or hazard logic. Those bits
add about 65 EX/MEM flops plus mux/load on the EX branch-target and rs2 data
paths, exactly where the post-DIV design is trying to preserve Fmax. Pruning
this redundant metadata should not change CoreMark cycles, bus behavior,
branch prediction, RVFI ordering, or any ISA-visible result.
hypothesis:
Remove redundant EX/MEM payload fields and reuse the already-carried RVFI
operand value for store data. Specifically, delete `write_data`,
`branch_taken`, and `branch_target` from `ex_mem_t`. In EX, stop assigning
those dead registers; keep the local branch_taken and branch_target signals
for redirect, misalignment checks, and pc_next generation. In MEM, form store
write data from `in.rs2_val`, which is the same post-forwarded rs2 value that
`write_data` carried before.

This is intended as a pure synthesis and placement micro-optimization:
fewer EX/MEM register bits, less fanout from the branch target adder and rs2
forwarding mux, and no new combinational datapath. The top-level core IO,
NRET=2 RVFI fields, channel-0-only retirement convention, decoder legality
discipline, iterative divider protocol, pending-store slot semantics, and
CoreMark instruction stream all remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove `write_data`, `branch_taken`, and `branch_target` from `ex_mem_t`
and update the bundle comments to state that `rs2_val` is both the
RVFI-visible rs2 operand and the raw store-data source before byte-lane
replication. Do not change any RVFI fields, top-level ports, ALU opcode
constants, or decoder legality definitions.

Stop driving the removed EX/MEM fields in the normal, divide-complete,
and bubble paths. Keep the existing local `branch_taken` and
`branch_target` calculations for redirect decisions, misaligned target
traps, and `pc_next`; only the unused registered copies are removed.
Preserve the divider interlock, forwarding muxes, JAL/JALR rd data, and
RVFI operand metadata.

Replace uses of `in.write_data` with `in.rs2_val` when building
replicated store write data and RVFI `mem_wdata`. This should be
bit-identical because EX already records the post-forwarded rs2 operand
in `rs2_val`. Preserve all existing misalignment traps, dmem request
gating, pending-store enqueue behavior, load extraction, and MEM/WB
retirement fields.

implementation_notes:

title: Stall-Only I-Fetch Replay Cache
motivation:
The current best core has already removed the cold DIV/REM datapath from
EX and added a one-deep pending-store slot, so the remaining structural
loss is less about store backpressure and more about the instruction side
of the tournament's random bus model. Today `io_imemReady=0` still becomes
a global frontend stall: the PC holds, IF emits a bubble, and decode cannot
use an instruction even if the same hot-loop PC was fetched many times
earlier.

The previous fetch skid queues tried to decouple live imem traffic from
decode, but they added permanent queue and PC-control logic on the zero-wait
path and lost too much Fmax. A better structural angle is a stall-only
replay cache: keep the current direct live-imem path whenever the external
bus is ready, and consult a tiny cached copy only on cycles where imem would
otherwise stall. CoreMark's timed code repeatedly executes the same matrix,
list, state, CRC, and loop-control instructions over a 10-iteration run, so
even a small direct-mapped instruction replay table should turn many random
iStall bubbles into normal fetch cycles after warmup.
hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped instruction replay cache inside IF, for example
256 entries indexed by `pc[9:2]` with valid bits, full PC tags, and 32-bit
instruction data. On any cycle where the live imem word is accepted
(`io_imemReady=1`), fill or refresh the entry for the current PC and use the
live `io_imemData` exactly as the current best design does. On a cycle where
`io_imemReady=0`, if the current PC hits in the replay cache, treat fetch as
ready and feed the cached instruction into the existing branch/JAL predictor,
decode path, and prediction metadata. If the PC misses while imem is not
ready, preserve the current stall and bubble behavior.

This keeps the zero-wait fast path structurally close to the current best
RTL: no extra fetch stage, no learned branch table, no load-data path into
EX, and no change to retirement width. Redirects and reset still flush the
in-flight IF/ID payload and restart the PC at the resolved target, but they
do not need to invalidate immutable instruction-cache contents. The hazard
unit should see an effective fetch-ready signal equal to `io_imemReady ||
replay_hit`, so cached hot instructions can advance through IF even during
random imem stalls. All RVFI fields continue to report the instruction that
actually flows down the pipeline, and single-retire channel 0 plus tied-off
channel 1 remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Add the direct-mapped stall-only instruction replay cache. Select
`io_imemData` on normal ready cycles and cached data only when
`io_imemReady` is low and the current PC tag hits. Fill entries on
ready live-imem fetches, clear valid bits on reset, keep redirects from
updating the architectural IF/ID payload, and run the existing
backward-branch plus direct-JAL prediction logic from the selected
instruction word.

Replace the raw imem-ready stall input with an effective fetch-ready
input generated by IF. A cache hit during an external imem stall should
not freeze PC or inject an IF bubble; a replay miss with `io_imemReady=0`
should preserve the current stall behavior. Keep load-use, divide-long,
redirect, and MEM wait interlocks unchanged.

Wire `io_imemReady` into IF, wire IF's effective fetch-ready output into
the hazard unit, and preserve the existing top-level core IO, pending
store slot arbitration, RVFI NRET=2 port set, strict channel-0 ordering,
and tied-off channel 1 behavior.

Clarify IF/ID prediction comments to note that instruction metadata may
come from live imem or from the stall-only replay cache. Do not change
bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality defaults, RVFI
fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## Stall-Only I-Fetch Replay Cache

### What changed

- Added a 256-entry direct-mapped replay cache in `rtl/if_stage.sv`, indexed by `pc[9:2]`, with valid bits, full 32-bit PC tags, and 32-bit instruction data.
- Live imem remains the selected instruction path whenever `io_imemReady` is high. The replay cache is selected only when live imem is not ready and the current PC tag hits.
- Filled/refreshed the replay entry on every live ready fetch. Reset clears valid bits; redirects do not invalidate cache contents.
- Ran the existing static backward-branch and direct-JAL prediction logic from the selected instruction word, whether live or replayed.
- Added `fetch_ready = io_imemReady || replay_hit` from IF and wired it through `rtl/core.sv` into `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`.
- Updated the hazard unit so only an effective fetch miss bubbles IF; replay hits during external imem stalls advance normally.
- Clarified IF/ID bundle comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to note that instruction and prediction metadata may come from live imem or replay.

### Deviations

- No new module was introduced. The replay cache lives inside `if_stage` to keep the fast path local and avoid another structural boundary.
- The in-place `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation was blocked by sandbox permissions when the script attempted to refresh generated files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher`. I ran the same staging/check sequence against the current `rtl/` from a writable `/tmp/rf-run` copy of riscv-formal instead.

### Concerns

- The cache advances the PC during external imem stalls on hits, so `io_imemAddr` can change while `io_imemReady` is low. That matches the tournament's ready/data model used here, but it is not a fully held-address bus protocol.
- The 256-entry table adds valid/tag/data storage in IF. It is intentionally only on the stall path for data selection, but the tag compare contributes to the effective fetch-ready logic.

### Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Formal equivalent to `bash formal/run_all.sh` staged under `/tmp/rf-run`: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Single-Adder Static Control Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor features are the simple ones that already paid
for themselves: backward conditional branches are predicted taken and direct
JAL targets are predicted in IF. The learned direction tables and return
predictors reduced CoreMark cycles slightly, but each added enough IF-state,
comparison, or EX-to-IF update routing to lose more Fmax than it recovered.

The remaining predictor opportunity is therefore to make the profitable
static predictor cheaper rather than adding more prediction state. Today
`if_stage` forms branch and JAL targets with two separate `pc + imm` adders
and uses the full adder results for target-alignment gating before choosing
the next PC. Since B- and J-type immediates always have bit 0 clear, the
target alignment test can be derived from the encoded imm[1] bit and the PC
low bits without waiting for a 32-bit target adder. Branch and JAL are also
mutually exclusive opcodes, so one selected-immediate target adder is enough.

This attacks predictor-path Fmax with no intended CoreMark cycle-count risk:
the set of predicted conditional branches and direct JALs remains the same
as the current best design for all aligned PCs, and EX still repairs any
wrong prediction through the existing resolved-next-PC mismatch path.
hypothesis:
Refactor the IF-stage static predictor into a single shared control-target
datapath. Decode the fetched instruction into `is_branch`, legal branch
funct3, `is_jal`, branch immediate, and JAL immediate as today, but select
one predictor immediate before the target add. Compute only one
`predicted_target = pc + predicted_imm` for the branch/JAL predictor rather
than building independent branch and JAL target adders in parallel.

Replace the current `branch_target[1:0] == 0` and `jal_target[1:0] == 0`
prediction guards with low-bit predecode: for B-type, target bit 1 is
`pc[1] ^ instr[8]`; for J-type, target bit 1 is `pc[1] ^ instr[21]`; bit 0
remains `pc[0]` because the immediate low bit is zero. Only predict when
those derived low target bits are word-aligned. Keep the current direction
policy exactly: legal backward conditional branches predict taken, direct
aligned JAL predicts taken, and JALR remains unpredicted.

No predictor table, BTB, RAS, update feedback, pipeline stage, top-level IO,
RVFI field, or retirement behavior changes. The expected win is a modest
median Fmax improvement from removing one IF target adder and taking the
adder result out of the prediction-enable select path, while preserving the
current best predictor's IPC behavior.

changes.description:
Replace the parallel branch-target and JAL-target predictor adders with
one selected-immediate target adder. Derive branch and JAL target
word-alignment from the PC low bits plus the encoded immediate bit 1
(`instr[8]` for B-type, `instr[21]` for J-type) instead of from the full
target-adder output. Preserve the existing reset/flush/redirect/stall
suppression, legal-branch guard, backward-branch-taken rule, direct-JAL
prediction, predicted_taken/predicted_target metadata, and JALR
unpredicted behavior.

Update only comments for the IF/ID and ID/EX prediction metadata to
document that the static predictor uses a shared branch/JAL target path
and low-bit predecoded alignment. Do not change bundle widths,
architectural state, RVFI fields, decoder legality rules, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
## What changed

- Refactored `rtl/if_stage.sv` so the static branch/JAL predictor forms a single `predicted_imm` and computes one shared `predicted_target = pc + predicted_imm`.
- Removed the parallel `branch_target` and `jal_target` predictor adders from IF.
- Replaced IF prediction alignment guards that consumed full target-adder outputs with low-bit predecode:
  - B-type target alignment uses `pc[0] == 0` and `(pc[1] ^ instr[8]) == 0`.
  - J-type target alignment uses `pc[0] == 0` and `(pc[1] ^ instr[21]) == 0`.
- Preserved the existing prediction policy and suppression rules: legal backward conditional branches predict taken, direct aligned JAL predicts taken, JALR remains unpredicted, and reset/flush/redirect/stall still suppress prediction.
- Updated only comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document the shared branch/JAL predictor target path and low-bit alignment predecode.

title: Segmented RVFI Order Counter
motivation:
The current best design has already captured the large architectural wins:
DIV/REM is off the one-cycle EX path, and the one-deep store slot reduces
random dmem-stall exposure. Recent micro-opt attempts that reshaped hot
datapaths, predictor target logic, or EX/MEM metadata mostly lost Fmax to
placement noise or new muxing. The next micro-opt should therefore target a
guaranteed always-present timing structure without changing IPC.

The top-level RVFI order counter is currently a single 64-bit increment:
`rvfi_order_q <= rvfi_order_q + 64'b1` on every retirement. The FPGA
benchmark wrapper intentionally retains RVFI fanout by XOR-reducing the RVFI
outputs into an LED, so this counter cannot be assumed dead in synthesis.
A 64-bit carry self-loop is not architectural work for the CoreMark datapath,
but it can still consume carry-chain/routing budget and compete with the hot
pipeline for median Fmax.
hypothesis:
Replace the monolithic 64-bit RVFI order increment in `rtl/core.sv` with a
segmented exact counter, for example four 16-bit chunks. The low chunk
increments on every `mem_wb_w.valid` retirement. Each higher chunk increments
only when all lower chunks overflow in the same retirement cycle. The
concatenated `{hi, mid_hi, mid_lo, lo}` value still drives
`io_rvfi_order_0`, resets to zero, and advances by exactly one for every
single channel-0 retirement.

This preserves CLAUDE.md invariant 4 bit-for-bit: no gaps, no duplicates,
and the visible 64-bit RVFI order sequence is identical to the current
implementation for every possible run length. It also leaves the top-level
IO, channel-1 tie-off, retirement point, pipeline control, memory behavior,
branch prediction, divider protocol, and CoreMark cycle count unchanged. The
expected gain is a modest median Fmax improvement by replacing one 64-bit
carry feedback path with shorter chunk-local adders plus narrow overflow
enables.

changes.description:
Replace the single `logic [63:0] rvfi_order_q` plus 64-bit `+ 1`
update with segmented order-counter registers. Increment the low
segment on retirement and conditionally increment upper segments on
lower-segment overflow. Drive `io_rvfi_order_0` from the concatenated
segments exactly as before, keep reset-to-zero semantics, and do not
change any RVFI field names, widths, channel ordering, channel-1 tie-off,
or MEM/WB retirement logic.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Replaced the monolithic `logic [63:0] rvfi_order_q` increment in `rtl/core.sv` with four 16-bit RVFI order counter chunks.
- The low chunk increments on every `mem_wb_w.valid` retirement.
- Each upper chunk increments only when all lower chunks are `16'hffff` on the same retirement cycle.
- `io_rvfi_order_0` is still driven by the concatenated 64-bit value, and reset still clears all chunks to zero.
- No top-level IO, channel-1 tie-off, MEM/WB retirement logic, pipeline control, memory behavior, or RVFI field widths/names were changed.

title: Narrow Stall-Only I-Fetch Replay Cache
motivation:
The current best core combines the two structural/timing wins that have
actually paid off: cold DIV/REM is off the one-cycle EX path, and the
one-deep pending-store slot hides store-only dmem stalls without hurting the
ready-store path. The remaining measured structural opportunity is on the
instruction side. The 256-entry stall-only replay cache cut CoreMark cycles
from 4,226,169 to 3,459,314, showing that random iStall bubbles are a large
part of the remaining IPC loss, but its wide IF table/tag/data mux dropped
median Fmax enough to miss the current best.

That result points to a narrower replay structure rather than a broader
fetch queue. CoreMark's timed hot code repeatedly executes compact inner
loops and call bodies, so a smaller direct-mapped replay table should retain
much of the iStall hit-rate benefit while reducing the IF storage mux,
comparator fanout, and PC-control load that hurt Fmax in the 256-entry
version. This keeps the zero-wait path direct and targets only cycles that
would otherwise be external imem stalls.
hypothesis:
Add a 64-entry direct-mapped stall-only instruction replay cache in IF,
indexed by `pc[7:2]`, with valid bits, a high-PC tag, and a 32-bit
instruction word. On `io_imemReady=1`, use the live `io_imemData` exactly as
the current best design does and refresh the indexed replay entry for the
current PC. On `io_imemReady=0`, consult the replay table; on a valid tag hit,
treat fetch as ready and feed the cached instruction into the existing
backward-branch/direct-JAL predictor and IF/ID metadata path. On a miss, keep
the current stall-and-bubble behavior.

This is deliberately smaller and less intrusive than the previous 256-entry
replay-cache experiment. The live ready path remains live-imem-first, no
fetch/decode queue or extra pipeline stage is added, and the replay data is
selected only for cycles that would otherwise stall. Redirects and reset
still flush the in-flight IF payload and restart the PC at the resolved
target, but immutable replay entries need not be invalidated except by reset.
The hazard unit should consume an effective fetch-ready signal
`io_imemReady || replay_hit`, preserving existing load-use, divide-long,
MEM-wait, pending-store-slot, branch recovery, RVFI, and single-retire
behavior.

changes.description:
Add the 64-entry stall-only replay table. Select live `io_imemData` on
ready cycles and cached instruction data only when `io_imemReady` is low
and the indexed PC tag hits. Fill entries from live ready fetches, clear
valid bits on reset, preserve the current redirect priority and static
backward-branch/direct-JAL prediction behavior, and emit an effective
fetch-ready output for the hazard unit.

Replace the raw imem-ready stall input with the IF-stage effective
fetch-ready signal. A replay hit during an external imem stall should
allow the PC/IF payload to advance normally; a replay miss should behave
like today's imem stall. Keep load-use, divide-long-operation,
redirect, and MEM wait interlocks unchanged.

Wire `io_imemReady` into IF and wire IF's effective fetch-ready output
into the hazard unit. Preserve the pending-store-slot arbitration, the
top-level core IO shape, RVFI NRET=2 port layout, strict channel-0
ordering, and tied-off channel 1.

Clarify only comments for the IF/ID prediction metadata to note that an
instruction may come from live imem or from the stall-only replay cache.
Do not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder legality
defaults, RVFI fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: Small Stall-Only I-Fetch Replay Predictor
motivation:
The predictor changes that have paid so far are the ones that avoid broad
learned direction state: backward conditional branches and direct JAL targets
are predicted in IF, while the larger branch tables and return predictors
lost more Fmax than their cycle savings recovered. The one exception with a
large cycle signal was the stall-only instruction replay experiment: it cut
CoreMark cycles substantially by serving hot-loop instructions during random
imem stalls, but its 256-entry full-tag table pulled median Fmax below the
current best.

That result suggests a narrower predictor, not a new branch-direction table.
CoreMark's timed region repeatedly executes a relatively small hot text
footprint, so a smaller replay predictor should retain much of the random
iStall hiding while shortening the IF tag/data muxes that hurt the 256-entry
attempt. This stays in the predictor slot because the table predicts the
instruction word for the current PC only when the external fetch bus would
otherwise provide no word; normal ready fetches still use live imem data.
hypothesis:
Add a 64-entry direct-mapped stall-only instruction replay predictor in
`if_stage`, indexed by `pc[7:2]`, with valid bits, full 32-bit PC tags, and
32-bit instruction data. On any cycle where `io_imemReady` is high, use the
live `io_imemData` exactly as the current best RTL does and refresh the
corresponding predictor entry. On a cycle where `io_imemReady` is low, if the
current PC's tag hits, treat fetch as effectively ready and feed the cached
instruction word into the existing backward-branch and direct-JAL predictor
logic. If the tag misses while imem is stalled, preserve the current PC-hold
and IF-bubble behavior.

The table is deliberately much smaller than the previous 256-entry replay
cache to reduce IF mux depth and tag-compare fanout. It also has no branch
update feedback, BTB target state, RAS bookkeeping, extra pipeline stage, or
load-data path into EX. Redirects and reset flush only the in-flight IF/ID
payload and restart the fetch PC; the replay contents are immutable copies of
prior live imem words and do not need redirect invalidation. All RVFI fields
still describe the instruction that actually enters the pipeline, and EX
keeps the existing resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery for branch/JAL
prediction mistakes.

`io_imemReady` is high; select cached data only when live imem is not
ready and the full tag matches the current PC. Fill entries on ready
live fetches, clear valid bits on reset, and run the existing static
backward-branch plus direct-JAL prediction from the selected instruction
word.

Replace the raw imem-ready stall input with an effective fetch-ready
input from IF. A replay hit during `io_imemReady=0` should not hold the
PC or inject an IF bubble; a replay miss should keep the current stall
behavior. Preserve load-use, divide-long, redirect, and MEM wait
interlocks exactly as in the current best RTL.

Wire `io_imemReady` into IF and wire IF's effective fetch-ready signal
into the hazard unit without changing the top-level core IO, pending
store-slot arbitration, RVFI NRET=2 port set, channel-0-only retirement,
strict `rvfi_order` behavior, or tied-off channel 1.

Clarify comments on IF/ID prediction metadata to note that the
instruction word may come from live imem or from the stall-only replay
predictor. Do not change bundle widths, architectural state, decoder
legality defaults, RVFI fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added a 64-entry direct-mapped stall-only instruction replay predictor in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- The replay table is indexed by `pc[7:2]` and stores a valid bit, full 32-bit PC tag, and 32-bit instruction word.
- Live `io_imemData` is always selected when `io_imemReady` is high, and the corresponding replay entry is refreshed on that cycle.
- When `io_imemReady` is low, a full-tag replay hit makes IF effectively ready and feeds the cached instruction through the existing static backward-branch and direct-JAL prediction logic.
- Replay misses keep the prior PC-hold and IF-bubble behavior.
- Replaced `hazard_unit`'s raw imem-ready input with IF's effective `fetch_ready`.
- Wired `io_imemReady` into IF and `if_fetch_ready` into the hazard unit in `rtl/core.sv`.
- Clarified the IF/ID bundle comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that `instr` can come from live imem or the stall-only replay predictor.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis plan.
- I did add a NOP fallback for the internal selected instruction word on replay miss to avoid propagating uninitialized replay data through predictor decode while the IF output is being flushed.

## Concerns

- The replay contents are not invalidated on redirects, matching the hypothesis. This assumes the instruction memory contents are immutable during execution, as in the current benchmark/formal environment.
- The replay table is implemented as simple register arrays. This keeps the design direct and small, but synthesis quality will decide whether the added tag/data path pays for itself at FPGA P&R.

title: Byte-Segmented RVFI Order Counter
motivation:
The current best result came from a pure timing micro-optimization: replacing
the monolithic 64-bit RVFI order increment with four 16-bit counter chunks.
That change did not alter CoreMark cycles, memory behavior, branch
prediction, divider latency, or retirement semantics, but it raised median
Fmax enough to become the new best fitness. This strongly suggests the
RVFI-order feedback path is still visible to synthesis and placement because
the FPGA wrapper keeps RVFI outputs alive.

The current implementation still has a 16-bit carry-chain self-loop in the
low order chunk, and each retirement toggles that chunk. A narrower exact
chunk size is the most direct follow-up in the micro_opt slot: it targets the
same measured timing structure that just paid off, avoids another datapath or
predictor rewrite, and should leave CoreMark iterations/cycle unchanged.
hypothesis:
Refine the RVFI order counter in `rtl/core.sv` from four 16-bit chunks to
eight 8-bit chunks. The lowest byte increments on every `mem_wb_w.valid`
retirement. Each higher byte increments only when every lower byte is
`8'hff` on that same retirement cycle. Concatenating the bytes still drives
the exact 64-bit `io_rvfi_order_0` value, reset still produces zero, and the
visible sequence still advances by exactly one per channel-0 retirement.

This preserves CLAUDE.md invariant 4 exactly: no skipped order values, no
duplicate order values, and no change to the single-retire channel-0 /
channel-1-tied-off RVFI contract. It also leaves the top-level IO, MEM/WB
retirement point, store slot, divider interlock, branch predictor, memory
handshake behavior, and CoreMark cycle count unchanged. The expected gain is
a small additional Fmax improvement by shortening the always-active low-order
counter carry path from 16 bits to 8 bits while keeping the upper-byte carry
enables narrow and exact.

changes.description:
Replace the four 16-bit `rvfi_order_*_q` registers and carry signals
with eight 8-bit order counter registers. Increment byte 0 on each
retirement and conditionally increment bytes 1 through 7 only on exact
lower-byte overflow. Drive `io_rvfi_order_0` from the concatenated bytes
in the same 64-bit order as today, preserve reset-to-zero behavior, and
do not change any RVFI field names, widths, channel ordering, channel-1

title: MEM-Stage Load Replay Buffer
motivation:
The current best core has already captured the two strongest proven wins:
cold DIV/REM is off the universal EX path, and the one-deep pending-store
slot hides store-only dmem backpressure while preserving the ready-store fast
path. The next structural bottleneck is therefore load-side dmem stalls. A
load whose word was recently read still freezes the whole pipeline whenever
`io_dmemReady` is low, even though CoreMark repeatedly touches a compact 6 KB
working set across list, matrix, state, stack, and CRC code.

Instruction replay experiments showed that serving repeated hot accesses
during random bus stalls can cut many cycles, but putting a wide replay table
in IF cost too much Fmax. A smaller load replay structure in MEM attacks the
same random-stall problem on the data side, where the lookup is off the
branch/JAL predictor path and does not add load data to the EX operand muxes.
This is a structural stall-hiding change, not a broader predictor or
arithmetic rewrite.
hypothesis:
Add a small normal-RAM load replay buffer in the MEM stage, for example a
16-entry direct-mapped table indexed by word address. Each entry holds a
valid bit, word tag, and the raw 32-bit memory word returned by a previous
legal aligned or byte/halfword load. On `io_dmemReady=1`, loads keep using
the live `io_dmemRData` exactly as today and refresh the replay entry for the
aligned word address. On `io_dmemReady=0`, a legal normal-RAM load may
complete from the replay buffer on a valid tag hit: suppress the external
dmem read, use the cached raw word for sign/zero extension and RVFI
`mem_rdata`, and do not assert `mem_wait`.

Replay is deliberately conservative. It is disabled while the pending-store
slot is valid, for MMIO/marker/UART addresses, for trapping or misaligned
accesses, and for stores. Normal-RAM stores invalidate or update the matching
replay entry before they retire or enqueue into the pending-store slot, so a
later replayed load cannot observe stale data. Replay misses preserve the
current dmem wait behavior exactly. The top-level IO, single-retire RVFI
channel-0 convention, tied-off channel 1, divider interlock, branch
prediction, and pending-store ordering all remain unchanged. The expected win
is lower CoreMark cycle count under the 22% random dStall model with much
less Fmax risk than MEM-to-EX load forwarding or IF replay caches.

changes.description:
Add a small direct-mapped normal-RAM replay table with valid, tag, and
raw-word data state. Provide lookup-hit/data outputs for legal load word
addresses, refresh entries from live dmem load responses, and invalidate
or byte-merge-update matching entries on legal normal-RAM stores. Reset
clears only valid bits.

Integrate the replay lookup into load handling. Select cached raw word
data only when `dmem_ready` is low, the access is a legal non-trapping
normal-RAM load, no pending store slot owns the port, and the replay tag
hits. On a replay hit, gate off `dmem_ren`, clear `mem_wait`, drive
load-data extraction and RVFI `mem_rdata` from the cached word, and keep
replay misses plus all nonbufferable accesses on the existing wait path.

Wire any replay-buffer module instance or MEM-stage sideband signals
needed for store invalidation/update and dmem request gating. Preserve
the existing pending-store-slot arbitration, top-level core IO, RVFI
NRET=2 port layout, strict channel-0 order sequence, and tied-off
channel 1 behavior.

Add only comments or compact helper typedefs if needed to document
normal-RAM replay eligibility and raw-word cache metadata. Do not change
architectural state semantics, decoder legality defaults, RVFI field
widths, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: Registered Lookahead I-Fetch Replay Predictor
motivation:
The current best predictor state is still the simple static frontend:
backward conditional branches and direct JAL targets are predicted in IF.
Broader learned branch tables and return predictors reduced a few control
bubbles, but their IF reads, compares, and EX update routing lost more Fmax
than the cycle savings recovered.

The only predictor-family experiment with a large cycle signal was the
stall-only instruction replay table. The 256-entry replay version reduced
CoreMark cycles from 4,226,169 to 3,459,314 by serving hot instructions
during random imem stalls, but its combinational tag/data lookup sat directly
on the current fetch-ready and predictor path and pulled median Fmax below
the current best. Smaller combinational replay tables kept much of the same
timing problem while giving up hit rate.

The most promising predictor angle is therefore not another direction table
or return predictor, but a timing-clean replay predictor: keep the large
iStall-hiding benefit, while moving the table lookup off the same-cycle IF
decision path.
hypothesis:
Add a registered lookahead instruction replay predictor in `if_stage`. Keep a
direct-mapped replay table, preferably 128 or 256 entries indexed by fetch PC,
with valid bits, full PC tags, and 32-bit instruction data. On every live
`io_imemReady=1` fetch, use `io_imemData` exactly as the current best RTL
does and refresh that PC's replay entry.

Separately, precompute and register the replay lookup for the PC expected to
be fetched on the next cycle. The current cycle should consume only the
registered `{hit, pc_tag, instr}` candidate for the current PC when
`io_imemReady=0`; it should not perform a fresh table read and tag compare in
the same combinational path that drives `fetch_ready`, branch/JAL prediction,
and the PC update. On a registered replay hit during an external imem stall,
treat fetch as effectively ready and feed the cached instruction into the
existing backward-branch and direct-JAL predictor metadata path. On a replay
miss, preserve the current PC-hold and IF-bubble behavior.

Redirects and reset still flush only the in-flight IF payload and restart the
PC at the resolved target; replay contents are immutable copies of prior live
imem words and only need reset invalidation. The registered candidate must be
invalidated or recomputed on redirect, reset, and held-PC cases so a stale
word can never enter the pipeline. This keeps correctness guarded by full PC
tags and the existing EX resolved-next-PC mismatch recovery, while targeting
the 256-entry replay cache's cycle gain with substantially less Fmax damage.

changes.description:
Add the direct-mapped replay table plus a registered lookahead result for
the current fetch PC. Select live `io_imemData` on ready cycles; select
cached data only when `io_imemReady` is low and the registered candidate
has a full-tag hit for the current PC. Fill entries from live ready
fetches, clear valid bits on reset, invalidate/recompute the candidate on
redirects and held-PC cycles, and run the existing static backward-branch
plus direct-JAL prediction from the selected instruction word.

Consume an effective fetch-ready signal from IF instead of raw
`io_imemReady`. A registered replay hit during an external imem stall
should not hold the PC or inject an IF bubble; a miss should behave like
the current imem stall. Preserve load-use, divide-long, redirect, and MEM
wait interlocks exactly as in the current best RTL.

Wire `io_imemReady` into IF and wire IF's effective fetch-ready output to
the hazard unit. Do not change the top-level core IO, pending store-slot
arbitration, RVFI NRET=2 port set, strict channel-0 order sequence, or
tied-off channel 1 behavior.

Clarify the IF/ID prediction metadata comments to note that the
instruction word may come from live imem or from a registered lookahead
replay predictor. Do not change bundle widths, architectural state,
decoder legality defaults, RVFI fields, or the NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

## Changed

- Added a 256-entry direct-mapped instruction replay table inside `rtl/if_stage.sv`, with valid bits, full 32-bit PC tags, and 32-bit instruction words.
- Added a registered lookahead replay candidate for the expected next fetch PC. Current-cycle IF selection only chooses between live `imem_data` and the previously registered candidate; it does not read or tag-compare the replay table combinationally.
